SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper states clear claims and provides experiments comparing an LSTM to an n-gram baseline using both manual annotations and an automated rhyme-density/maximum-similarity evaluation, which supports the core claims, but important reproducibility and validation details are missing. The summary indicates the dataset was heuristically filtered and may contain non-verse text, code release is not mentioned, and there is no indication that statistical significance testing or ablations were performed to rule out confounds (e.g., tuning on test data or dataset leakage). Mathematical content appears minimal/appropriate for the domain, but the automated metric’s limitations are acknowledged and not fully mitigated. Given these gaps, the technical contribution is promising but not yet fully validated for robust reproducibility or definitive conclusions. | B(4): The paper's principal claims are generally supported by experiments and analysis: a detailed manual annotation protocol, a workable automated extension of Potash et al. that penalizes repetition, and a new artist-annotated corpus are all presented and used to compare an LSTM to an n-gram baseline. There are no mathematical proofs to check, and the methodology is largely sound, but the evaluation relies on a single relatively weak baseline (n-gram) and the summary does not report statistical testing or robustness checks, so some claims about generalization of the LSTM are not fully substantiated. The authors acknowledge key limitations (e.g., automated metrics not capturing vocabulary/tone and preprocessing heuristics), which mitigates but does not eliminate concerns about overclaiming.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper states clear claims and provides experiments comparing an LSTM to an n-gram baseline using both manual annotations and an automated rhyme-density/maximum-similarity evaluation, which supports the core claims, but important reproducibility and validation details are missing. The summary indicates the dataset was heuristically filtered and may contain non-verse text, code release is not mentioned, and there is no indication that statistical significance testing or ablations were performed to rule out confounds (e.g., tuning on test data or dataset leakage). Mathematical content appears minimal/appropriate for the domain, but the automated metric’s limitations are acknowledged and not fully mitigated. Given these gaps, the technical contribution is promising but not yet fully validated for robust reproducibility or definitive conclusions.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's principal claims are generally supported by experiments and analysis: a detailed manual annotation protocol, a workable automated extension of Potash et al. that penalizes repetition, and a new artist-annotated corpus are all presented and used to compare an LSTM to an n-gram baseline. There are no mathematical proofs to check, and the methodology is largely sound, but the evaluation relies on a single relatively weak baseline (n-gram) and the summary does not report statistical testing or robustness checks, so some claims about generalization of the LSTM are not fully substantiated. The authors acknowledge key limitations (e.g., automated metrics not capturing vocabulary/tone and preprocessing heuristics), which mitigates but does not eliminate concerns about overclaiming.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper provides an adequate set of experiments — manual annotations (fluency, coherence, style matching), automated rhyme-density and maximum-similarity measures, a 13-artist dataset, and a direct LSTM vs n-gram comparison — which supports the central claims at a basic level. However, it lacks ablation or sensitivity analyses (e.g., contributions of parts of the automated metric, effects of preprocessing or model hyperparameters) and does not report code release (preprocessing was heuristic and may leave non-verse text), which hampers exact reproducibility despite reported hyperparameters. Related work coverage is reported as adequate and limitations are discussed, but the experimental depth and reproducibility safeguards are only moderate, so the work is solid but not fully comprehensive. | B(4): The paper presents a substantial empirical effort: a comprehensive manual evaluation protocol, an improved automated stylistic metric (rhyme density + max similarity), a new 13-artist corpus with manual style annotations, and experiments comparing an LSTM to an n-gram baseline showing LSTM generalization. It appropriately discusses limitations (automated metrics miss vocabulary/tone/themes; preprocessing heuristics may leave non-verse text) and reports hyperparameters, but ablation/sensitivity analyses on the automated metric and model variants are limited and code availability is unclear, which somewhat weakens full reproducibility and diagnostic insight.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper provides an adequate set of experiments — manual annotations (fluency, coherence, style matching), automated rhyme-density and maximum-similarity measures, a 13-artist dataset, and a direct LSTM vs n-gram comparison — which supports the central claims at a basic level. However, it lacks ablation or sensitivity analyses (e.g., contributions of parts of the automated metric, effects of preprocessing or model hyperparameters) and does not report code release (preprocessing was heuristic and may leave non-verse text), which hampers exact reproducibility despite reported hyperparameters. Related work coverage is reported as adequate and limitations are discussed, but the experimental depth and reproducibility safeguards are only moderate, so the work is solid but not fully comprehensive.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents a substantial empirical effort: a comprehensive manual evaluation protocol, an improved automated stylistic metric (rhyme density + max similarity), a new 13-artist corpus with manual style annotations, and experiments comparing an LSTM to an n-gram baseline showing LSTM generalization. It appropriately discusses limitations (automated metrics miss vocabulary/tone/themes; preprocessing heuristics may leave non-verse text) and reports hyperparameters, but ablation/sensitivity analyses on the automated metric and model variants are limited and code availability is unclear, which somewhat weakens full reproducibility and diagnostic insight.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper tackles a concrete and underexplored evaluation problem for creative language generation by providing a comprehensive manual protocol, an improved automated rhyme/novelty metric, and a manually annotated corpus for 13 rap artists — resources that materially help reproducibility and benchmarking in this niche. However, its impact appears moderate: the contributions are valuable to researchers working on lyric or style-conditioned generation but are specialized to rap lyric ghostwriting and constrained by known limitations (limited automated coverage of vocabulary/tone, heuristic preprocessing, and lack of broader evaluation modalities). Overall it opens useful avenues and datasets for follow-on work but is unlikely to fundamentally change broader NLP evaluation practices on its own. | B(3): The paper addresses an important practical problem—reliable evaluation of creative language generation—by providing a comprehensive manual annotation protocol and an improved automated metric suite (rhyme density plus penalized similarity) tailored to rap lyric ghostwriting, and it releases a 13-artist corpus annotated for stylistic similarity. These contributions are valuable and likely to be adopted by researchers working on stylistic generation and evaluation, and they open concrete follow-up work (better automated style metrics, cross-genre validation). However, the impact is somewhat constrained by the narrow domain (rap lyrics), reliance on heuristic preprocessing of OHHLA data, and limited evidence of generalization beyond the LSTM vs. n-gram comparison, so I judge the overall significance as moderate rather than field-changing.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles a concrete and underexplored evaluation problem for creative language generation by providing a comprehensive manual protocol, an improved automated rhyme/novelty metric, and a manually annotated corpus for 13 rap artists — resources that materially help reproducibility and benchmarking in this niche. However, its impact appears moderate: the contributions are valuable to researchers working on lyric or style-conditioned generation but are specialized to rap lyric ghostwriting and constrained by known limitations (limited automated coverage of vocabulary/tone, heuristic preprocessing, and lack of broader evaluation modalities). Overall it opens useful avenues and datasets for follow-on work but is unlikely to fundamentally change broader NLP evaluation practices on its own.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper addresses an important practical problem—reliable evaluation of creative language generation—by providing a comprehensive manual annotation protocol and an improved automated metric suite (rhyme density plus penalized similarity) tailored to rap lyric ghostwriting, and it releases a 13-artist corpus annotated for stylistic similarity. These contributions are valuable and likely to be adopted by researchers working on stylistic generation and evaluation, and they open concrete follow-up work (better automated style metrics, cross-genre validation). However, the impact is somewhat constrained by the narrow domain (rap lyrics), reliance on heuristic preprocessing of OHHLA data, and limited evidence of generalization beyond the LSTM vs. n-gram comparison, so I judge the overall significance as moderate rather than field-changing.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This paper addresses a topic squarely within NLP creative-generation and evaluation, proposing a comprehensive manual protocol and an improved automated metric (penalizing repetition) and releasing a 13-artist lyric corpus — all appropriate for a top-tier NLP audience. The methods and metrics (rhyme density, maximum similarity, line-level fluency/coherence, page-level style matching) and the LSTM vs. n-gram comparison match community evaluation standards, though lack of mentioned code release and some preprocessing caveats weaken reproducibility. Overall the problem formulation and positioning are well aligned with venue expectations, with only minor concerns about reproducibility and the limited baseline set. | B(4): This paper is a good fit for a top NLP/ML venue: it addresses creative language generation with a substantive methodological contribution (manual evaluation protocol plus an improved automated style-matching metric) and a released artist-annotated corpus—materials that will interest the community. The focus on rap ghostwriting is a well-chosen case study that surfaces general evaluation challenges (fluency, coherence, style/novelty tradeoffs) likely to generalize beyond the domain, though the niche application and reliance on a single generative baseline (n-gram vs. LSTM) create minor scope concerns. Overall the evaluation standards, metrics, and dataset curation align with expectations for the venue, but stronger demonstration of generalizability or broader baselines would make the fit exemplary.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper addresses a topic squarely within NLP creative-generation and evaluation, proposing a comprehensive manual protocol and an improved automated metric (penalizing repetition) and releasing a 13-artist lyric corpus — all appropriate for a top-tier NLP audience. The methods and metrics (rhyme density, maximum similarity, line-level fluency/coherence, page-level style matching) and the LSTM vs. n-gram comparison match community evaluation standards, though lack of mentioned code release and some preprocessing caveats weaken reproducibility. Overall the problem formulation and positioning are well aligned with venue expectations, with only minor concerns about reproducibility and the limited baseline set.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper is a good fit for a top NLP/ML venue: it addresses creative language generation with a substantive methodological contribution (manual evaluation protocol plus an improved automated style-matching metric) and a released artist-annotated corpus—materials that will interest the community. The focus on rap ghostwriting is a well-chosen case study that surfaces general evaluation challenges (fluency, coherence, style/novelty tradeoffs) likely to generalize beyond the domain, though the niche application and reliance on a single generative baseline (n-gram vs. LSTM) create minor scope concerns. Overall the evaluation standards, metrics, and dataset curation align with expectations for the venue, but stronger demonstration of generalizability or broader baselines would make the fit exemplary.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper compares the proposed LSTM primarily against an n-gram baseline and incorporates an automated variant of Potash et al. (2015), which shows some historical context; however, it lacks stronger contemporary neural baselines (e.g., other RNN/seq2seq or Transformer-based models) that would be expected for a 2015+ era study. Comparisons appear fair in that they use the same OHHLA-derived corpus and shared evaluation metrics (manual fluency/coherence/style matching and automated rhyme-density/maximum-similarity), and the paper acknowledges limitations of automated evaluation and LSTM weaknesses. Overall the comparison is adequate but incomplete for claiming broad superiority given the missing modern baselines and absence of code/reproducibility details in the summary. | B(3): The paper evaluates an LSTM generator against an n-gram baseline and extends a prior semi-automatic evaluation (Potash et al., 2015) into a fully automated metric, which shows reasonable engagement with earlier work. However, the empirical comparisons are limited: only an n-gram baseline is used rather than stronger, more recent neural baselines (e.g., seq2seq/Transformer-based models), so the set of competing methods is incomplete. Comparisons appear fair in that they use the same OHHLA-derived corpus and the proposed manual and automated metrics, and the authors acknowledge limitations of their automated evaluation relative to aspects other methods might capture. From an impact and generalizability perspective, the paper analyzes differences between the LSTM and n-gram results, but the absence of contemporary baselines constrains claims about substantive improvements over prior generation approaches.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares the proposed LSTM primarily against an n-gram baseline and incorporates an automated variant of Potash et al. (2015), which shows some historical context; however, it lacks stronger contemporary neural baselines (e.g., other RNN/seq2seq or Transformer-based models) that would be expected for a 2015+ era study. Comparisons appear fair in that they use the same OHHLA-derived corpus and shared evaluation metrics (manual fluency/coherence/style matching and automated rhyme-density/maximum-similarity), and the paper acknowledges limitations of automated evaluation and LSTM weaknesses. Overall the comparison is adequate but incomplete for claiming broad superiority given the missing modern baselines and absence of code/reproducibility details in the summary.

However, the empirical comparisons are limited: only an n-gram baseline is used rather than stronger, more recent neural baselines (e.g., seq2seq/Transformer-based models), so the set of competing methods is incomplete. Comparisons appear fair in that they use the same OHHLA-derived corpus and the proposed manual and automated metrics, and the authors acknowledge limitations of their automated evaluation relative to aspects other methods might capture. From an impact and generalizability perspective, the paper analyzes differences between the LSTM and n-gram results, but the absence of contemporary baselines constrains claims about substantive improvements over prior generation approaches.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper makes useful but largely incremental contributions: it operationalizes a comprehensive manual evaluation protocol for rap ghostwriting and converts a previously semi-automatic Potash et al. (2015) method into a fully automated, repetition-penalizing metric, and it releases a 13-artist OHHLA-derived corpus with manual style annotations. These are practical, reproducible advances (valuable to the subfield) but do not introduce a fundamentally new problem formulation or radically novel modeling technique—the modeling comparison is an LSTM vs. an n-gram baseline. An expert would be informed by the dataset and improved evaluation, but would not be surprised by the methodological choices or the scale of innovation. | B(3): The paper offers a domain-focused and useful contribution—formalizing a manual evaluation protocol for rap ghostwriting, releasing a 13-artist lyrics corpus, and making a previously semi-automated Potash et al. (2015) measure fully automated with repetition penalties. These advances are practical and valuable for the creative-generation community but are largely incremental: they adapt and extend existing evaluation ideas rather than introduce a fundamentally new framework or modeling approach. From my perspective as a creative-generation researcher, the clear differentiation from prior work and the curated dataset increase utility, but the methods themselves are non‑trivial extensions rather than breakthrough innovations.

An expert would be informed by the dataset and improved evaluation, but would not be surprised by the methodological choices or the scale of innovation.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the paper appears well organized with a logical flow from methodology to experiments and conclusion, and its main contributions (manual evaluation methodology, automated extension, and release of an annotated corpus) are stated early and clearly. Notation and metrics are generally well-defined (rhyme density, maximum similarity, manual fluency/coherence/style matching) though the summary suggests some heuristic preprocessing details that may need clearer specification for exact reproducibility. Figures, tables, and examples are reported as effective and writing quality is good, but the discussion of automated-evaluation limitations and missing mention of released code slightly weaken practical clarity for replication. | B(4): The paper is well-organized and follows a logical flow from introduction through methods, experiments, and conclusion, and its main contributions (manual evaluation methodology, automated extension, and a curated lyric corpus) are clearly stated early. Notation and methodological descriptions appear consistent and sufficiently defined for the described metrics (rhyme density, maximum similarity, manual fluency/coherence/style matching). Figures, tables, and examples are reported as effective and the writing quality is good, though the presentation could more explicitly clarify preprocessing heuristics and the limits of automated evaluation (e.g., vocabulary/tonal aspects) in a few places. Overall the manuscript is clear and readable with only minor presentation issues.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall the paper appears well organized with a logical flow from methodology to experiments and conclusion, and its main contributions (manual evaluation methodology, automated extension, and release of an annotated corpus) are stated early and clearly. Notation and metrics are generally well-defined (rhyme density, maximum similarity, manual fluency/coherence/style matching) though the summary suggests some heuristic preprocessing details that may need clearer specification for exact reproducibility. Figures, tables, and examples are reported as effective and writing quality is good, but the discussion of automated-evaluation limitations and missing mention of released code slightly weaken practical clarity for replication.

main_claims: A comprehensive manual evaluation methodology for rap lyric ghostwriting that assesses fluency, coherence, and style matching.
An improved, fully automated version of a previously semi-automatic evaluation method (from Potash et al., 2015) that penalizes repetitive text.
Creation and release of a dataset/corpus of lyrics for 13 rap artists, manually annotated for stylistic similarity.
Demonstration of the evaluation methodology by analyzing an LSTM-based ghostwriter and showing the LSTM generalizes better than an n-gram baseline in automated evaluation.
_summary.methodology: Combine manual annotation of generated verses along line-level fluency and coherence and page-level style matching with an automated evaluation that uses rhyme density and maximum similarity regressions to assess stylistic similarity and novelty; compare an LSTM generative model to an n-gram baseline.
datasets: OHHLA-derived lyrics corpus for 13 rap artists (collected from The Original Hip-Hop (Rap) Lyrics Archive - OHHLA.com)
limitations: Automated evaluation cannot assess certain aspects such as vocabulary, tone, and themes of an artist.
The LSTM model has difficulty integrating new vocabulary, limiting desirable similarity scores.
Preprocessing was heuristic and the resulting dataset may still contain non-verse text (dialogue or chorus) despite filtering.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - creative language generation (rap lyric ghostwriting)

reviewer_a.name: Experimental Reproducibility Engineer
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer with a PhD in computational linguistics; focuses on rigorous experimental design, evaluation metrics, and reproducible implementations for language-generation research.
traits: insists on clear definitions of metrics, their units, and how they are computed (including edge cases)
looks for thorough baselines, ablation studies, and statistical significance testing to support claims
values release of code, seeds, and data preprocessing scripts to enable exact replication
checks for potential confounds (e.g., dataset leakage, train/test contamination, hyperparameter tuning on test data)
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Practical and detail-oriented: evaluates whether the experiments actually support the claims and whether others can reproduce the results.

reviewer_b.name: Creative-Generation Researcher
reviewer_b.background: Associate professor in NLP with a track record in creative language generation and evaluation; studies evaluation methodologies and the societal impacts of generative systems, especially in creative domains like poetry and music.
traits: assesses novelty relative to prior work and whether the methodological contribution meaningfully advances evaluation practice
examines the choice of domain (rap ghostwriting) for broader insights and whether findings generalize beyond the case study
appraises dataset curation choices, annotation scheme quality, and ethical/impact considerations (e.g., artist attribution, misuse)
values interpretability and diagnostic analyses that link evaluation metrics to human judgments
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Contextual and impact-focused: judges the paper by its novelty, generalizability, and potential influence on the field and downstream applications.
